translation.content: Scroll through end-of-year features or the tracklists of a certain kind of new-school techno DJ, and Priori is rarely far away. The Montreal artist has built a reputation as a kind of studio chameleon, working with the biggest names across the underground. Whether his collaborations with james K, Tiga or Paul St. Hilare, or his work co-running naff recordings, his output will be familiar to any raver who has touched grass at the likes of Sustain-Release, Dekmantel or Waking Life in recent years. The "Priori touch" is easy to spot. It shapeshifts as you listen: strange, synthetic textures, enveloping low-end, everything draped in a fine silk gauze that seems to hover just above the surface. But for all its hallmarks, it's also deeply versatile. Priori is prog, Priori is dub, sometimes Priori is even pop. Just as you think you've grasped his sound, it slips away. This is by design. Priori takes inspiration from not only myriad genres, but mediums, too. He's just as likely to be moved by an obscure illbient 12-inch from a dusty record shop as, say, a Wong Kar-wai film. "I love world-building," he once told Butter Sessions, and that instinct lends his productions a sense of richness. Making his long-overdue debut on the RA Mix, Priori opens the door to the chillout room. RA.1031 is packed full of, to borrow from the unmistakable words of CCL, "sexy oven" material, built on thick, enveloping bass and a constant sense of atmospheric drift. Take one standout passage: Paul St. Hilaire's voice into Trickpony into Kassem Mosse, before everything dissolves under the weight of Andy Stott's "Luxury Problems." It's a perfect microcosm of the Priori world: dub, prog and electronica, all folded together with ease. Who knows what thrilling new forms await in the rest of the decade. – Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? Travelling for shows, trying to find balance between touring, working on music and being there for the people I love. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? On my laptop, while in transit. I've been listening to a lot of slower stuff, somewhere between club music, illbient and electronica. Most of my recent mixes have been club recordings, so I tried to do something a bit different here, blending unexpected things together and embracing dissonance. Mood over functionality. What are you looking forward to in the near future? Taking a break and spending some time at home reading, cooking, being in nature with my girlfriend. Also putting together a new live set for Jump Source, a project I do with Patrick Holland that we'll be touring in 2026 after our first LP comes out in April via naff recordings. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? I think most causes are interconnected. Loads of people are being displaced right now—welcome them. Offer love and empathy. Cultivate strong local communities. It makes us stronger against authoritarianism, more resilient in the face of corporate greed and climate change. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? Sustain-Release was very formative for me. I've seen so many sets there that have shaped the way I DJ and play live. The set I played with Cousin in 2024 was so unexpected and memorable, and it made me realise I prefer off-peak sets. The music I truly resonate with shines outside of regular club hours.
translation.blurb: A key architect of the 2020s underground debuts on the RA Mix.
